interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SCLC SCOPE Project Volunteer and Staff Member; Western States College Coordinator
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program; Library of Congress; Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

main_summary.summary:
William S. ('Willie') Leventhal recounts how a Jewish kid born in New York in 1946 and raised in Los Angeles housing projects, North Hollywood, and the South Bay came to volunteer with Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s Southern Christian Leadership Conference in the summer of 1965. He traces his early consciousness to anti-Jewish slurs (kike) directed at him in the Catholic neighborhoods where he grew up, to the Jackie Robinson story film he saw at age five, and to his parents' NAACP membership and his maternal grandfather Morris Segal's earlier support for the Scottsboro Boys defense. As a UCLA freshman, Leventhal heard King personally recruit students at UCLA for the Summer Community Organization and Political Education Project (SCOPE), and after initially holding back out of fear, he was confronted by a Jewish high-school friend and signed up.

Leventhal describes the orientation week at Morehouse College in Atlanta -- where he heard Dr. King, Bayard Rustin, Hosea Williams, Septima Clark, Vernon Jordan, John Hope Franklin, C. Vann Woodward, Charles Morgan Jr., Clarence Mitchell Jr., Michael Harrington, and John Doar -- and then his field assignment to Macon, Georgia, working under local SCLC leader William P. Randall (Bibb County) and SCOPE administrator Joel Siegel. He recounts arrests in Macon (after being booked then quickly released through Randall's intervention with the police chief) and in Americus, Georgia, where he was sent as reinforcement for Hosea Williams's 'hot movement' following the killing of bystander Andrew Whatley and the jailing of four Black women. In Americus he was arrested with John Lewis attempting to integrate the First Baptist Church (confronted by fire chief Henderson) and led a group to the First Methodist Church the following Sunday when Willie Bolden asked him to lead so Bolden would not be arrested. He was shot at twice from close range -- once with passenger Rich Adler in Fred Hoffman's Citroen while attempting to organize a maids' union, and again the following day while walking with Black companions.

After SCOPE's formal end on August 28, 1965, Leventhal joined SCLC's field staff, stayed three extra weeks in Macon for the September 15 election in which Reverend Marcus Moon ran for commissioner, then returned to UCLA following a 24-hour death threat from an anonymous caller. Back in California he served as SCLC's Western States college coordinator under Gwen Green at the Western Christian Leadership Conference (WCLC, led by Rev. Thomas Kilgore Jr.). In fall 1966 a confrontation with his Santa Monica landlord over Black children visiting his apartment escalated into a 112-day jail stay at LA County Jail, where the chief jail psychiatrist Marcus Crane (the same psychiatrist who later certified Sirhan Sirhan as sane) tried unsuccessfully to have him committed. Leventhal closes by criticizing Pulitzer Prize-winning King biographers Taylor Branch and David Garrow for relying on disputed FBI files and for omitting SCOPE from the historical record; by reflecting on post-traumatic stress as a likely root of SNCC resentment toward SCLC; and by celebrating reconciliation with former SNCC members Willie Ricks and Sammy Mahone, the Sumter County movement, and the SCOPE/SNCC families who hosted him.
key_themes: Voter registration and the Voting Rights Act of 1965
Post-traumatic stress as legacy of movement work
Historiography of the civil rights movement (Branch and Garrow critique)
main_summary.historical_significance: Leventhal is one of approximately 500 SCOPE volunteers who fanned out across 120 Southern Black Belt counties in summer 1965 under Hosea Williams's direction, registering tens of thousands of new Black voters before passage of the Voting Rights Act. His testimony documents an understudied SCLC program -- omitted from Eyes on the Prize and from Taylor Branch's America in the King Years trilogy -- and provides firsthand witness to the Americus, Georgia crisis (Andrew Whatley's killing, the church integration attempts with John Lewis, the Sumter County jailings) and to the network of local Black leaders such as William P. Randall in Macon and Sammy Mahone in Americus. His account also bears on post-1965 SCLC western operations through the Western Christian Leadership Conference in Los Angeles.

errors: Date of orientation week not explicitly confirmed in summary text (transcript places it after recruitment by King at UCLA following the Selma-to-Montgomery marches, but summary frames timing implicitly)

Summary describes Marcus Crane as the psychiatrist who certified Sirhan Sirhan as sane; transcript supports this but the exact characterization ('certified as sane' vs. 'said Sirhan needed to be locked up in a mental hospital' as Leventhal phrases it elsewhere) collapses two contrasting Leventhal statements -- the summary uses a paraphrase that risks compressing his actual framing

Summary names 'fire chief Henderson' at the First Baptist Church; transcript does say 'fire chief Henderson came and we left,' but at one point Leventhal also says the fire chief had a tape recorder and the encounter occurred at what 'I think it was the Baptist church' -- the summary states this as definite when Leventhal himself hedged the denomination once
improvements: Add explicit dates where transcript provides them (interview took place on Saturday, April 13 -- year not stated in transcript text)
Clarify that Leventhal sometimes hedged on details (e.g., which church first; whether bullets were real or blanks) rather than presenting his account as fully certain

Add a sentence noting that some claims (e.g., FBI's role in his Santa Monica arrest, the existence of Stokely Carmichael's recorded apology to Dr. King's memory) are Leventhal's interpretation rather than independently verifiable in the transcript

chapters.title: Childhood in LA, Family, and Early Influences
chapters.summary:
Leventhal opens by establishing the interview context (Malibu, California; April 13; with interviewer David Cline and videographer John Bishop on behalf of the Southern Oral History Program, the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture, and the Library of Congress). He recounts being born in New York in 1946 to a father (Dr. Bernard Leventhal, a chiropractor and World War II veteran) and a mother (a special education teacher who later became a USC professor). The family moved to California when he was two and lived briefly in Ramona Gardens, a federal housing project, around 1948, where his mother worked on the campaign of Edward R. Roybal. He describes his maternal grandfather Morris Segal, assistant superintendent of New York City schools, who handled veterans' vocational education after World War II.

Leventhal narrates the formative impact of seeing the Jackie Robinson story film at age five -- linking baseball, racial prejudice, and the experience of being called 'kike' by Catholic kids in his South Bay neighborhood. He describes his parents' divorce, his confirmation at age 15, and the friendship with high school athlete Mike Vogel (who became first team All City in football and baseball at Westchester High). He invokes Jewish athletic role models (Hank Greenberg, Sandy Koufax, Al Rosen, Norm Sherry, Larry Sherry) as cultural anchors. He also names John F. Kennedy as a defining figure of his generation, citing Kennedy's June 1963 civil rights address (the night before Medgar Evers was killed).
related_events: Brooklyn Dodgers move to Los Angeles

improvements: Could add more specific dates from the transcript where given (e.g., the 1948 housing project residence)
Could note the religious/cultural particularity of his confirmation more explicitly

chapters.title: Recruitment by Dr. King at UCLA
chapters.summary:
Leventhal describes his political awareness in 1964-1965, including reading about the March on Washington (which he did not attend because he was 16 and preparing for high school football), and the killings of three civil rights workers (two of whom were Jewish) in Mississippi the summer of Freedom Summer. He recounts his first clear personal memory of Martin Luther King's work: King's January 1, 1965 entry into Selma at the invitation of local educators and Amelia Boynton, and an argument with his then-girlfriend's stepfather (a Navy veteran with a drinking problem) defending King's voter-registration focus.

In the wake of the Selma campaign and Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge -- which Hosea Williams co-led with John Lewis -- Dr. King came to UCLA approximately a month and a half later, announcing the SCOPE Project. Joel Siegel (then humor editor of the Daily Bruin, later Good Morning America entertainment editor) was a key organizer; Richard 'Rick' Tuttle, then 25, served as on-campus advisor; Alex White ran the UCLA service organization. Leventhal initially hesitated to volunteer (citing being 'just a freshman' and fear of being killed like the Mississippi workers), but was confronted and persuaded by a Jewish friend with whom he had played football. He also wrote a high school editorial about a Black athlete denied housing during the Proposition 14 controversy in 1964. He notes the death of his mother (from lung cancer three years later) and the stress his decision placed on his family.
related_events: Bloody Sunday at the Edmund Pettus Bridge
Dr. King's recruitment speech at UCLA

errors: Transcript describes the Mississippi killings as 'two are Jewish' -- the summary accurately captures this but does not name James Chaney, Mickey Schwerner, and Andy Goodman; later in the transcript Leventhal does name them in connection with Queens College
improvements: Reference Andy Goodman, Mickey Schwerner, and James Chaney by name where the speaker eventually does
Note that King came to UCLA in 1965 -- the transcript specifies that this was 20 months after the March on Washington which Leventhal did not attend

chapters.title: Conversion Experience and SCOPE Project Architecture
chapters.summary:
Leventhal describes his racial 'conversion experience' in the South. He had previously thought of himself as treating all people equally, but argues that one cannot do so without immersion in the Black community because in cultural and physical detail, Black people simply look different. He cites the parallel testimony of another SCOPE worker from Carlisle, Pennsylvania, and Sherry LeBetus and Maria Gitin (a University of Alabama scholar writing about scope workers after his organizing of the first SCOPE reunion). Within weeks of immersion, Leventhal recalls, Black people stopped looking different to him and White people started looking different -- the white people 'literally wanted to kill me and did try and kill me' while Black people were his friends and baseball teammates.

He then outlines SCOPE's structure. The SCLC board, at Hosea Williams's request, approved the project in December 1964, targeting 120 Black Belt counties chosen not as the worst but as those where an additional 30-40% Negro voter registration could displace the worst racists. Williams, a World War II veteran of General Patton's 'Negro Tank Brigade,' approached the project as a battle plan -- which Leventhal contrasts with the Lowndes County Freedom Organization / Black Panther approach (where there were no Black voters at all). SCLC sought 2,000 volunteers but only got 500. Leventhal notes that more civil rights workers were killed in 1965 than in 1964, citing Andrew Whatley's death in Americus and the Deacons for Defense and Justice in Louisiana.

errors: Transcript says SCOPE was approved 'in December' without specifying year 1964; summary infers 1964 from context. This is accurate but extrapolated.
improvements: Could note that Leventhal hedges (Andrew Young 'has told me that those same 120 scope counties all went for Barack Obama and may have made the difference in the election 2008') -- his claim about Obama is reported speech, not independently verified

chapters.title: Orientation Week at Morehouse College
chapters.summary:
Leventhal describes the SCOPE orientation week at Morehouse College (with volunteers housed in the dorms). He calls it 'the best week of education in American history.' Speakers included Dr. King, Bayard Rustin (who had organized the March on Washington), A. Philip Randolph, Hosea Williams, Norman Hill (a CORE veteran serving as Rustin's assistant), C. Vann Woodward and John Hope Franklin from academia, Lillian Smith (present but not speaking), Septima Clark (who founded the citizenship education schools), Vernon Jordan, Charles 'Chuck' Morgan Jr. from the ACLU, Clarence Mitchell Jr. (NAACP), Michael Harrington (author of The Other America), and John Doar of the Justice Department. Leventhal recalls that Dr. King's closing speech -- adapted from his 'free at last' formulation -- specifically named Muslims and Hindus alongside Jews and Gentiles and Black and White Protestants and Catholics, drawing on his recent travel in India.

Leventhal also recounts a SCOPE worker named Larry Scott Butler being sent to Bogalusa, Louisiana, and the host family man with a gun stating he would defend his daughter even though SCOPE was nonviolent. He cites Andrew Young's argument that without the nonviolent discipline of SCLC and SNCC the South would have looked like Northern Ireland, since most rural Southerners owned guns from World War II and Korean War service. He also names Clifford and Virginia Durr and Aubrey Williams as courageous white Alabamians who supported the movement throughout.
related_events: SCOPE orientation week at Morehouse College, June 1965

errors: Transcript mentions 'A Jewish man who was head of the meat packers union' as also present at orientation, but Leventhal said he could not recall the man's name -- the summary omits this attendee, which is fine but worth flagging
improvements: The summary correctly cites King's 'Muslims and Hindus' phrasing; could note this is corroborated by Leventhal's claim that the recording is in Morehouse College archives

chapters.title: Assignment to Macon, Georgia and Local Leadership
chapters.summary:
Leventhal narrates SCOPE's deployment from Morehouse to field assignments. He was assigned to Bibb County, Georgia (Macon) -- one of the largest UCLA groups -- because of strong local leadership under William P. Randall. He describes Randall's network, which included Tom Jackson (the only Black lawyer in Macon at the time, who became the first Black judge there) and Lewis Wynn. Joel Siegel served as administrative leader for the Macon SCOPE office. Leventhal contrasts SCOPE's organizational chain of command (Hosea Williams as 'general,' Willie Bolden as enforcer) with SNCC's more horizontal model. He notes that Larry Scott Butler's Brandeis-anchored group was redirected from an original assignment to Clayton, Alabama in Barbour County (George Wallace's home county) to 'raise hell on George Wallace,' and recounts Wallace allegedly remarking that they were the 'best dressed civil rights workers I ever saw' -- consistent with SCOPE's instruction to dress preppy, not have beards, and be careful with interracial couples in public so as not to inflame whites.

Leventhal recounts living with the Simmons family in East Macon (Lucille, MMA Simmons the professional cook, and son Terrell Simmons, who later played for the Macon Bombers baseball team). He describes recruiting Black youth including Willie Bowens (who later worked at the VA) and William C. Randall (William P. Randall's nephew) from neighborhoods around Macon. He also describes the Stanford Project SELF, whose records he says are at Stanford's Green Library, in which Joel Siegel singled him out as 'the hardest worker' on the Macon team.

errors: Transcript says 'Janet Merritt was a woman who was a state rep after the gang' regarding Americus -- summary omits this; not a primary error but worth flagging the secondary characters elided
improvements: Could mention more specifically that William P. Randall was a Bibb County Commissioner and his son was a state rep, his granddaughter is a state rep, and his son is a judge -- the legacy chain is in the transcript

chapters.title: Baseball with the Macon Bombers
chapters.summary:
Leventhal devotes substantial attention to playing for the Macon Bombers, an all-Black local team (also called the Bibb Bombers) during summer 1965 -- making him 'the only white Macon Bombers' player. He describes how Willie Bowens, a teammate, brought him out for a tryout at Round Oak, Georgia, in a cow-pasture baseball field 40 miles from Macon. He played five games, was on base 11 of 15 times (with 10 walks and 1 hit-by-pitch in Warner Robins), made two errors (one at Warner Robins on an Air Force base game), and his teammate Ralph Eason hit a monster home run out of the stadium lights at Warner Robins to redeem one of his errors. He praises teammate Jim Goethe (later a convert to Judaism, who played for the Kansas City organization, ran annual baseball clinics in Macon for 25 years).

He details the historical context: Brooklyn Dodgers (when Brooklyn Dodgers held spring training in Vero Beach) used to insist on playing integrated exhibition games in Macon, Birmingham, Montgomery, and Jacksonville on their way north, so when the Dodgers played Macon's Luther Williams Field (built 1919, the third-oldest baseball field), Jackie Robinson and Roy Campanella played there. Luther Williams Field was used for filming dugout scenes in the 2013 Jackie Robinson biopic '42.' Leventhal also recounts that in 2002 he helped organize a Macon Bombers reunion, including ceremonies through the Georgia Sports Hall of Fame and the local police department (one officer recognized him as a former civil rights worker).
related_events: Warner Robins Air Force Base baseball game
42 (Jackie Robinson film, 2013) filming at Luther Williams Field

errors: Transcript says Luther Williams Field was the 'third oldest' field; summary correctly preserves this hedge
Summary describes the Warner Robins game as on 'an Air Force base game'; transcript supports this (military bases were integrated by law since 1948)
improvements: Could note that Leventhal's claim about being 'the only white Macon Bombers player' is his own framing; the team's other rosters before/after his brief stint are not addressed
Main_topic_category is debatable -- 'Music & Culture' is the closest standard category but 'baseball' is not 'music' -- it is cultural integration content; this is the best fit but marginal

chapters.title: Arrests in Macon and the Citroen Shooting
chapters.summary:
Leventhal recounts two arrests in Macon. The first occurred when he and his SCOPE roommate Ken Long were soliciting registrations from bars on Broadway (now Martin Luther King Drive). A police officer arrested Leventhal in a barbershop where he had gone to call the office (per John Doar's training to call in any police incidents). The officer cited Leventhal as a 'nigger lover' and 'smart aleck' as the reason. William P. Randall promptly negotiated his release with the police chief, who agreed to drop the charges if Leventhal would consent -- which he did. The second Macon arrest involved William C. Randall (the local leader's 16-year-old nephew with an expired learner's permit) crashing Ken Long's car into a parked vehicle in a Black man's yard; Leventhal was charged for allowing an unlicensed minor to drive, and a $60-65 fine was paid by his father.

Leventhal then describes Americus, Georgia, where he was sent as reinforcement for Hosea Williams's 'hot movement' following Andrew Whatley's killing. He recounts being shot at twice. The first incident: he and SNCC volunteer Rich Adler (a Jewish gymnast from Grant High School in the LA Hall of Fame) were driving Fred Hoffman's Citroen -- the only Citroen in Georgia, marking them as outsiders -- attempting to organize a maids' union. A pickup truck followed them through the white downtown; as it pulled alongside, a gun was extended from the passenger window and fired three times. No bullet holes were found, leaving Leventhal uncertain whether they were blanks. The second incident occurred the following day while walking with four Black companions when a truck fired at him and he dove into the bushes; he heard the bullet hit foliage and confirmed it was real.
related_events: Klan attempts on civil rights workers
Maids' union organizing effort in Americus
Bibb County Macon arrest of SCOPE worker
First Baptist Church integration attempt with John Lewis

errors: Transcript hedges on whether the bullets in the Citroen incident were real or blanks; summary correctly notes uncertainty
improvements: Could note that the Macon fine was reported as '$60.5' or '$60-65' -- transcript is ambiguous
Could clarify that Rich Adler is described as 'somewhere in other' having joined SNCC -- Leventhal does not know exactly which college

chapters.title: John Lewis at the Baptist Church and Sumter County Context
chapters.summary:
Leventhal recounts being paired with John Lewis in Americus on a Sunday to attempt to integrate the First Baptist Church. Fire chief Henderson came out with a tape recorder; he told Lewis they did not want him or Leventhal there, and when Lewis -- a former seminarian -- began quoting scripture, Henderson became flustered and ordered them to leave. The encounter was on national news with CBS, NBC, and ABC coverage. The following Sunday, Leventhal led a group to the First Methodist Church when Willie Bolden asked him to lead because Bolden could not afford to be arrested as a movement leader. After being denied entry and asking to say a prayer (which they were granted), the group was arrested. The photo of this arrest ran on the front pages of the LA Times, Atlanta Constitution, and New York Times. Inside the jail, Sheriff Fred B. Chappell separated whites from 'NIGRAs,' which Lewis identified as a violation of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 -- though Chappell told him he might run the streets but did not run the jail.

Leventhal frames Americus through people: Sammy Mahone (a SNCC leader in Americus who later co-chaired the Americus-Sumter County Movement), Gloria Wise (one of four women arrested for standing in a white women's voting line, sister of Jewel), Mary Bell Peacamble, and Lena Turner (a SNCC worker who had written to every white church in Americus after the Civil Rights Act of 1964). He describes Albert 'Red' Heffner of the Community Relations Service, a McComb Mississippi insurance man whose daughter had been Miss Mississippi and whose family was forced to leave McComb (the subject of Hodding Carter III's book 'So the Heffners Left McComb'). Heffner's superior LeRoy Collins (former governor of Florida, an Andrew Young associate) ran the federal community relations service.
related_events: Civil Rights Act of 1964 enforcement in southern jails
Albert Heffner Community Relations Service work
Four women jailed in Americus (Wise, Peacamble, Turner, Mahone)

errors: Transcript names the book about the Heffners as 'So the Heffners Left McComb' by 'the hot ink harder, the third' -- this is a transcription corruption of 'Hodding Carter III'; summary uses the corrected name based on standard civil rights historical knowledge

Transcript spells one of the women arrested as 'Amy Peacamble'; summary uses 'Mary Bell Peacamble' (a variant) -- this is potentially an error; the transcript actually has 'Amy Peacamble' so summary's 'Mary Bell' may be inaccurate. NOTE: This is a potential supplied/incorrect detail -- needs correction
improvements: Correct 'Mary Bell Peacamble' to 'Amy Peacamble' as the transcript states
Could note that the book attribution to 'Hodding Carter III' is canonicalized from the transcript's garbled rendering

chapters.title: Post-Traumatic Stress, the Election, and Death Threat
chapters.summary:
Leventhal describes returning to Macon for an extra three weeks to work on the September 15, 1965 election in which Reverend Marcus Moon -- a Macon barber and minister -- ran for commissioner (the first Black candidate since Reconstruction in Macon). On election day, the city changed every polling place and printed the directions in fine print -- a precedent Leventhal cites as proof that the Voting Rights Act of 1965's enforcement provisions remain necessary. The night of the election, he received an anonymous phone call: a voice asked if he was the white man carrying 'niggas down to vote today,' then said 'if you're still here in 24 hours, you're dead.' William P. Randall insisted on personally driving him to the airport the next morning.

Leventhal frames his return through the lens of post-traumatic stress: as he drove the Long Beach Freeway from LAX two days later, he found himself unable to tolerate cars passing him. He describes seeing the UCLA campus psychologist, who diagnosed it as Peace Corps-style adjustment difficulty rather than danger-related PTSD. He extends this analysis to SNCC as an institution, arguing that SNCC's hostility toward SCLC stems in part from unprocessed post-traumatic stress and depression. He calls out specific reconciliations: Stokely Carmichael near the end of his life telling Louis Farrakhan not to attack Dr. King, and Willie Ricks ('white boy, we don't want you here' in Americus, May 1965) eventually approaching him as a friend in 2007. He praises Sammy Mahone as a model of inclusive movement memory.
related_events: Watts riots (timing relative to Voting Rights Act of 1965)

errors: Leventhal's claim about Stokely Carmichael telling Farrakhan not to attack Dr. King is presented as hearsay in the transcript ('It's on video. I've not seen it, but I've heard of it.') -- the summary correctly preserves the hearsay framing
improvements: Could add 'according to Leventhal' framing around the SNCC post-traumatic stress diagnosis, as this is his interpretation

chapters.title: Western States Coordinator and Santa Monica Confrontation
chapters.summary:
Leventhal recounts being appointed Western States college coordinator for SCLC by Gwen Green, executive director of the Western Christian Leadership Conference (WCLC, the SCLC's western branch covering Washington, Oregon, California, Nevada, Arizona, Colorado, New Mexico, Utah, Alaska, and Hawaii at 2400 Southwestern Avenue, Suite 102, Los Angeles, California 90018). Rev. Thomas Kilgore Jr. (formerly of New York) was president of WCLC. Marvin T. Robert is named as Marvin Stovall, chairman of the board. Leventhal helped organize WCLC's Operation Breadbasket-style work in Los Angeles black neighborhoods (parallel to Jesse Jackson's Chicago work). He met James Bevel during this period.

In fall 1966, an incident with three Black children visiting his Santa Monica apartment escalated. The landlord (whom Leventhal later learned was an ex-Santa Monica policeman) reported that someone had threatened to bomb the apartment because Leventhal had Black guests. Following his rabbi's advice to verify the threat, and police advice to buy a gun for self-defense, Leventhal bought a shotgun. When he called the landlord to verify the threat, the landlord called the police, who in turn arrested Leventhal -- though he was carrying the (broken-open) shotgun. He tried to file a report; the police refused; he was eventually arrested in a phone booth at Santa Monica and 26th Street. After he resisted being touched by the arresting officer, the situation escalated into a fight at the booking station; four officers slammed his head on the ground until he passed out.

errors: Leventhal gives two slightly different versions of the WCLC address: '2401 Southwestern Avenue' and '2400 Southwestern Avenue, Suite 102, Los Angeles, California 90018' -- summary uses the later, more specific version, but this is a transcript inconsistency
The transcript renders the board chairman's name as 'Marvin T. Robert' and 'Marvin Stovall' in adjacent sentences -- these may be two people or a transcription error. Summary preserves the ambiguity
improvements: Note that Leventhal's account of the LA police escalation reflects his subjective experience; some details (e.g., the gun being broken open) are his framing

chapters.title: 112 Days in LA County Jail and Critique of King Historiography
chapters.summary:
Leventhal recounts being held 112 days at LA County Jail. The jail's chief psychiatrist Marcus Crane -- the same psychiatrist who two years later said Sirhan Sirhan was sane after killing Robert F. Kennedy -- wrote a hostile evaluation seeking Leventhal's commitment to a mental hospital under the Department 95 (psychiatric hold) procedure. Dr. Ronald Markman, then in his 30s and a UCLA-affiliated jail psychiatrist (who became a famous jail psychiatrist), repeatedly cleared Leventhal as not mentally ill, telling him to 'never hit a cop and particularly never at a booking room.' Leventhal was eventually sentenced to six months in jail, then released on time served with outpatient counseling, after his father told prosecutor Herbert Lapine 'over my dead body' regarding life imprisonment. During incarceration Leventhal was attacked by a Nation of Islam-affiliated inmate named Hugo Maxi using a razor-blade-embedded bar of soap, requiring stitches in his eye.

The chapter closes with Leventhal's critique of Pulitzer Prize-winning King biographers Taylor Branch and David Garrow. He argues that Garrow's 'Bearing the Cross' and Branch's 'America in the King Years' trilogy rely on disputed FBI files that the House Assassinations Commission report (held at UCLA Library) explicitly warned were 'a shameful chapter' of unverified attributions. He alleges that Cass Canfield at Harper & Row, and Holt, Rinehart and Winston, wanted Leventhal's own book to include 'sex things' which he refused -- a refusal he credits with his book not winning a Pulitzer. He also describes how Branch and Garrow systematically omit the SCOPE Project from their books, paralleling the exclusion of SCOPE from Eyes on the Prize. He frames Branch as a 'coward' who only sat in jail briefly in 1969 and who omitted Harris Wofford's name in connection with the Kennedy-Coretta Scott King phone call.
related_events: House Assassinations Commission report (19 volumes at UCLA Library)
Pulitzer Prize for 'Bearing the Cross' (David Garrow)
Pulitzer Prize for 'Parting the Waters' (Taylor Branch)
Eyes on the Prize documentary series
Kennedy-Coretta Scott King phone call October 1960

errors: Leventhal characterizes Marcus Crane's view of Sirhan as 'sane' but says elsewhere Crane 'said Sirhan needed to be locked up in a mental hospital. Because I worked for Martin Luther King.' These two statements are contradictory -- the summary's 'sane after killing Robert F. Kennedy' framing leans on one Leventhal claim while collapsing the contradiction
Leventhal's critique of Taylor Branch and David Garrow is presented as fact in the summary, but it is Leventhal's argument and contested by mainstream civil rights historiography
Leventhal claims Stokely Carmichael (Kwame Ture) made an apology video late in life telling Farrakhan not to attack Dr. King -- this is hearsay he has not personally verified
improvements: Frame Leventhal's critiques of Branch and Garrow more clearly as his polemic, not as fact
Resolve or flag the contradiction in Leventhal's two statements about Marcus Crane's view of Sirhan Sirhan

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SCLC SCOPE Project Volunteer and Staff Member; Western States College Coordinator
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program; Library of Congress; Smithsonian NMAAHC

evidence: Strong opening anchoring the interview to a specific date and place (Segment 5, 00:00:13,000): 'today is Saturday, April 13th, where Malibu, California'
Clear narrative arc from childhood (Segment 17, 00:01:02,000) through recruitment at UCLA (Segment 85, 00:13:15,000) to field work and jail (Segments 553-1015)
Frequent tangents into family history, baseball, and movement historiography that complicate the chronological backbone (e.g., Segment 71, 00:10:30,000 on Hank Greenberg and Sandy Koufax)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Leventhal has a clear chronological backbone (childhood -> UCLA -> Macon -> Americus -> return to LA -> jail) but his digressions into baseball, family history, Jewish identity, and grievances with historians repeatedly fragment the structure. The interviewer's prompts help bring him back but the overall flow requires patience.

most_vivid_moment.quote: Gun came out about this far away. I mean, I'm here. I'm the driver. The passenger car pulls up. The passenger sticks the gun out, bang, bang, bang.

evidence: Segment 122, 00:19:27,000: 'when I was arrested in America with John R. Lewis and others of the UCLA group were with us, the pictures are in the book, front page news'

Segment 252, 00:43:56,000: detailed orientation roster naming Bayard Rustin, A. Philip Randolph, Hosea Williams, Norman Hill, C. Vann Woodward, John Hope Franklin, Lillian Smith, Septima Clark, Vernon Jordan, Charles Morgan, Clarence Mitchell, Michael Harrington

Segment 285, 00:50:41,000: 'my first baseball game with the Macon Bombers in Round Oak, Georgia, which was in the middle of a cow pasture that had been, trees had been cut down, and there were cow patties on the baseball field'
vivid_details_examples.justification: Exceptionally rich in specific names, places, and reconstructed scenes. Leventhal can summon dozens of full names, addresses (e.g., 2400 Southwestern Avenue Suite 102), exact case numbers (Penal Code 653 MAPC), and reconstructed dialogue. The scene-level detail (Citroen shooting, jail psychiatric evaluation) is unusually granular even for a long oral history.

most_authentic_moment.quote: I have to watch myself now that when cars are behind me, I have to call myself down and say, what's going on? This is your kicking end, just relax. It's not 1965.

vulnerability_elements: Acknowledging the stress his decisions placed on his cancer-stricken mother (Segments 160-161)
Admitting his Macon arrest happened because 'he made a quick recovery' on a baseball play that should have been an error (Segment 395)
emotional_authenticity.justification: Leventhal is candid about his fear during the shootings, his post-traumatic stress symptoms decades later, and his family conflicts. However, his emotional disclosures are often mixed with grievance-narrative against Taylor Branch, David Garrow, and UCLA, which can undercut the vulnerability with defensiveness.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Leventhal's narrative is exceptionally detailed and personally vulnerable in places, but his structural coherence is undermined by frequent digressions and grievance loops. The peak emotional moments (the Citroen shooting, the jail head-slamming, the death-threat phone call) are genuinely powerful, but they are surrounded by extended argumentative passages about historiography that may exhaust listener engagement.

major_events: SCOPE Project orientation week at Morehouse College (Segments 247-275)
Americus, Georgia movement following Andrew Whatley killing (Segments 553-637)
First Baptist Church integration attempt with John Lewis (Segments 587-595)
First Methodist Church arrest with Willie Bolden's group (Segments 614-621)
September 15, 1965 Marcus Moon election in Macon (Segments 729-739)

key_figures.name: Hosea Williams
key_figures.relationship: Direct SCOPE supervisor; later close friend

key_figures.name: John Lewis
key_figures.relationship: Co-arrestee at First Baptist Church Americus

key_figures.name: William P. Randall
key_figures.relationship: Local Macon SCLC leader and host

key_figures.name: Willie Ricks
key_figures.relationship: SNCC counterpart in Americus, later reconciled

organizational_involvement.organization: Martin Luther King Jr. Federal Holiday Commission
organizational_involvement.role: Author of report to President Reagan and Congress

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: One of approximately 500 SCOPE volunteers and one of the few who joined SCLC's paid staff afterward; close associate of Hosea Williams; later author of the first book-length history of SCOPE (Scope of Freedom). Possesses primary documents including SCOPE incident reports, FBI investigation files, and his own arrest records.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Leventhal occupied an unusual position: an LA-based Jewish college freshman who became a paid SCLC staff member, then served as a federal holiday commission report author for Coretta Scott King. His direct contact with Hosea Williams, John Lewis, and Andy Young is well-documented, and his eyewitness account of the Americus crisis is corroborated by photographs in his book and CBS/NBC/ABC news footage he describes.

precision_examples: Segment 803, 02:23:43,000: 'August 28th... the official date of the conclusion' for SCOPE 1965
Segment 729, 02:09:54,000: 'September 15th in Macon' for the Marcus Moon election
Segment 908, 02:40:50,000: '2400 Southwestern Avenue, Suite 102, Los Angeles, California, I know 018' for WCLC headquarters
Segment 969, 02:51:53,000: 'a 653 MAPC of the penal code' for the disputed Santa Monica charge
Segment 999, 02:56:53,000: '112 days' jail time at LA County Jail
verifiable_claims: Joseph Mosnier interviewer name (corroborated by other Civil Rights History Project records)
Stanford Project SELF interview records (claimed at Stanford Green Library)
FBI investigation of 33 gas stations after John Doar referral (Segments 256-257)
Bibb County Commissioner role for William P. Randall (Segment 351)
specificity_of_details.justification: Many precise dates, addresses, and case numbers are provided. Names are predominantly full names with affiliations. Several claims can be cross-referenced against published records (his book Scope of Freedom; SCOPE archives; the Stanford SELF interviews; LA Times and NYT archive front pages).

new_information: Detailed Americus 1965 chronology bridging Andrew Whatley's killing, the four women's jailings, and the church integration attempts -- an underdocumented summer in major civil rights synthesis
Account of SCOPE's omission from Eyes on the Prize and the Branch/Garrow trilogies
Personal testimony to the WCLC operation in LA, an SCLC western branch rarely covered in published histories
Eyewitness on the network of Macon Black local leaders (William P. Randall, Tom Jackson, Lewis Wynn)
Insider account of the post-PTSD readjustment problem in the movement, generalized to SNCC vs SCLC tensions
understudied_aspects: Macon Bombers as an integrated baseball venue in 1965
The Heffner family forced exile from McComb, Mississippi
Jewish-Black civil rights alliance from the volunteer-personnel side
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Leventhal's account complicates two dominant narratives: (1) that 1965 movement activity was concentrated in Selma and Birmingham, by detailing the parallel Black Belt voter-registration push in Georgia counties under SCOPE; and (2) that SCLC's western operations were merely fundraising appendages, by describing WCLC organizing.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of SCLC's internal organization, of 1965 Black Belt voter registration outside Mississippi/Alabama, of the Jewish-Black alliance, and of post-movement psychological aftermath would find this interview useful as a primary source, especially in combination with Leventhal's published book and the Stanford SELF records he cites.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: The interview makes substantive contributions to several understudied corners of civil rights history, especially SCOPE Project institutional memory and the Americus 1965 crisis. However, much of the Branch/Garrow critique is interpretive rather than evidentiary, and Leventhal's claim that SCOPE counties carried Obama in 2008 (cited only as Andy Young's claim) is suggestive but unsubstantiated in the transcript itself.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Leventhal's historical value is high. He occupied multiple insider positions (SCOPE volunteer, SCLC paid staff, WCLC coordinator, Holiday Commission report author) and his eyewitness specificity is unusual. His main interpretive contribution -- the argument that SCOPE has been systematically erased from the canonical King narrative -- is itself a historiographical claim worth engaging seriously by movement historians.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Leventhal traces a transformation from fearful, sheltered Jewish freshman afraid of being killed like the Mississippi Freedom Summer workers, to a SCOPE volunteer who confronts gunfire and 24-hour death threats, to a SCLC staff member committed to a year of full-time movement work, to a PTSD-affected returnee who turns his energy toward LA organizing and later toward documenting the historical record.
key_turning_points: Confrontation by his Jewish friend who shamed him into volunteering (Segment 118, 00:18:53)
The conversion experience of immersion in Black community (Segments 180-208)
The Citroen shooting and post-traumatic stress (Segments 711-728)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Before: scared freshman who 'kept thinking of reasons' not to volunteer; saw himself as nonprejudiced because he thought he 'looked at everybody the same'. After: SCLC staff member who learned that you cannot look at everybody the same without being immersed in Black community; long-term PTSD sufferer who still cannot let cars pass him on the highway.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: His framing of fear-conquering is genuine: the 'I had no excuse except I'm scared' moment, followed by his actual immersion, is inspiring. His later refusal to take a plea deal even after his father pressured him is also a moral-conviction moment that could inspire.
transformation_growth.justification: The transformation arc is genuine and well-articulated, though Leventhal's emphasis on post-traumatic stress as ongoing rather than resolved gives the arc a bittersweet quality. His growth is partly intellectual (understanding SNCC vs SCLC dynamics through PTSD framework) and partly relational (reconciling with Willie Ricks).

peak_moments.description: Police car arrest in Macon, uncertain whether they would drive to jail or 'out to the country' to be beaten
peak_moments.stakes: Possible beating or death; outcome that hinged on a left or right turn
Three gunshots at point-blank range; uncertainty about whether bullets were real

peak_moments.description: Death-threat phone call after Marcus Moon election
peak_moments.stakes: 'If you're still here in 24 hours, you're dead'

peak_moments.description: LA County Jail attack by Hugo Maxi with razor-blade soap

tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From fear and panic during the shootings, to outrage at the LA arrest, to grief over his mother's death, to bitter resentment at Branch/Garrow, to warm reconciliation with Willie Ricks and Sammy Mahone
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Multiple high-stakes dramatic moments are described with specific detail. The Macon arrest, the Americus shootings, the death threat, and the LA jail attack all carry palpable tension. The dramatic arc is occasionally diluted by long digressive passages, but the peaks are strong.

universal_themes: Fear and the choice to act anyway
Generational tension over a child's risk-taking
The desire to be remembered in history
relatable_struggles: Being teased as an outsider (kike slurs as a child)
Wanting to fit in with athletic peers
Fighting bureaucratic and legal injustice as an individual
human_moments: Calling his father to say he was going south, and his father saying 'I'm proud of you' (Segment 586, 01:41:20)
Worry about his mother's lung cancer and the stress his arrests placed on her (Segments 160-161)
The umpire's mercy call on his first baseball play with the Macon Bombers (Segments 396-401)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Universal themes of fear-overcoming and family stress are accessible. However, much of the interview's middle and latter sections involve detailed grievances against specific historians, named UCLA officials, and SNCC veterans that are quite context-specific and may not resonate with general audiences.
relatable_experiences.justification: Several broadly relatable moments are present (the father call, the umpire's grace, the kike-slur childhood), but the dominant register of the second half of the interview is grievance and historiographical argument, which limits broader accessibility.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Emotional resonance is strong in the action-and-stakes moments (shootings, jail, death threat) and in family-relational moments (father's pride, mother's illness), but is undermined by extended digressions into intramovement and intramurally-historian disputes that general audiences may find difficult to follow or care about.

barriers: Heavy use of insider names without identification (e.g., 'Hannah was there,' 'Bill said' often without first establishing which Bill)
Repeated assumption of listener familiarity with SCOPE, SNCC, WCLC, and SCLC abbreviations
Long sentence fragments and self-corrections suggesting impromptu rather than scripted delivery
language_clarity.justification: Leventhal is verbally articulate and free of dialect barriers, but his speech is densely populated with names, organizations, addresses, and case numbers that he does not pause to identify. New audiences would benefit from supplementary annotation; experienced civil rights historians can follow easily.

high_energy_sections: Segments 553-637 (Americus crisis and church integration attempts) sustains high tension
slow_sections: Segments 167-186 (extended commentary on gay marriage terminology) is a digression from the civil rights arc
Segments 825-895 (extended polemic against Taylor Branch and David Garrow) is heavy historiographical argumentation
Segments 326-447 (baseball detail) is engaging but extended beyond what most audiences need
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Three-hour transcript; the first 90 minutes hold attention well; the last 90 minutes require strong interest in civil rights history specifically because of the lengthy historiographical disputes and personal grievance passages.
narrative_flow.justification: The flow is genuinely uneven. Action segments are gripping; digressive segments are taxing. The interviewer (David Cline) intermittently brings Leventhal back to chronology but does not consistently shape the narrative arc.

themes_connected: Civil rights as American democratic foundation
Voting Rights Act of 1965 enforcement persistence
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Leventhal explicitly connects his story to contemporary debates over Voting Rights Act of 1965 enforcement provisions and qualified immunity reform, citing his own subsequent civil rights case in Iowa. He also frames the Arab Spring (citing Obama's Cairo speech) as a case study in nonviolence with weaker institutional foundations than the US movement.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Leventhal does make explicit meaning, but often defensively (about SCOPE's omission from canonical accounts) rather than expansively (about what younger generations should learn).
broader_themes_connection.justification: Some strong contemporary connections (Voting Rights Act of 1965 enforcement, qualified immunity, Arab Spring as nonviolence test case), but these are embedded in grievance arcs that limit their broader accessibility.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Accessibility is moderate. Leventhal is articulate and rich in detail, but the interview's three-hour length, frequent name-dropping without identification, extended historiographical disputes, and digressive structure make it most accessible to specialists. General audiences would need substantial editing or annotation.

overall_assessment: This is a long, dense, content-rich oral history from one of approximately 500 SCOPE Project volunteers in summer 1965. Leventhal's combination of insider position, primary-source documentation, and articulate vulnerability makes the interview historically valuable, particularly for the Americus 1965 crisis, SCLC's Western branch, and the SCOPE Project as institutional history. The interview is hampered by length, frequent digressions, and extended grievance passages against King biographers Taylor Branch and David Garrow that may dilute its broader accessibility. For specialist historians of civil rights, Jewish-Black alliance work, and post-movement psychology, however, it is a substantial primary source.
key_strengths: Exceptional first-person specificity (full names, exact addresses, case numbers, dates) that supports cross-referencing with other primary sources
Direct testimony to underdocumented events (Americus 1965 crisis, SCOPE Project organization, WCLC western operations) that fills genuine gaps in civil rights historiography
Genuine emotional vulnerability about PTSD and family stress that humanizes the movement experience
key_limitations: Three-hour length with frequent digressions and tangents that make sustained engagement difficult for general audiences
Extended historiographical grievances against Taylor Branch and David Garrow that, while substantive, occupy disproportionate interview time and present interpretive claims as established facts

claims.claim: William S. ('Willie') Leventhal was born in New York in 1946.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 18 (00:01:15): 'Born in New York in 46, but my dad had just gotten back from World War II.'

claims.claim: His father was Dr. Bernard Leventhal, a chiropractor and World War II veteran.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 24 (00:01:52): 'Still being in the service, he could fly free out to California and Bernard, Dr. Bernard Leventhal' name.' Segment 19 (00:01:23): 'He had a very good chiropractic practice in Saginaw, Michigan when he went in the war.'
claims.rationale: Both name and profession directly stated.

claims.claim: His mother was a special education teacher who later became a USC professor.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 20 (00:01:29): 'My mother, who was a teacher and later a college professor at USC, a special ed'

claims.claim: The family lived in Ramona Gardens, a federal housing project, around 1948.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 30 (00:02:31): 'we lived in Ramona Gardens, which was a federal housing project, probably about 1948'

claims.claim: His mother worked on the campaign of Edward R. Roybal.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 31-33 (00:02:36-00:02:58): 'My mother worked in the campaign of the first Hispanic councilman and then later congressmen... Roybal, Edward R. Roybal.'

claims.claim: His maternal grandfather Morris Segal was assistant superintendent of New York City schools.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 36 (00:03:08): 'my grandfather had been the assistant superintendent of New York City Schools, Morris Segal'

claims.claim: Leventhal saw the Jackie Robinson story film at age five.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 56 (00:06:19): 'the Jackie Robinson movie, Jackie Robinson story, which I saw at age five'

claims.claim: Dr. King came to UCLA to recruit for the SCOPE Project.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 85 (00:13:15): 'And then Dr. King came to UCLA when I was 18 and asked for volunteers.' Segment 86 (00:13:22): 'And now the summer community organization and political education project, which was known as scope, SCOPE project, and made the National Nelson at UCLA.'

claims.claim: Joel Siegel was a key UCLA organizer, then humor editor of the Daily Bruin, later Good Morning America entertainment editor.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 120-132 (00:19:07-00:22:11): 'a Joel Siegel was one of the Rick key organizers'; 'Joel Siegel at that time was the humor editor of the Daily Bruin'; 'I later became Joel Siegel, I'm good morning America entertainment editor.'

claims.claim: Richard 'Rick' Tuttle was the on-campus advisor, age 25.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 121 (00:19:17): 'Richard "Rick" Tuttle, another key organizer... he was an old man of 25 at that time. We were all 18 and 19 and 20.'

claims.claim: Alex White ran the UCLA service organization.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 156-157 (00:27:08-00:28:00): 'Rick me had mentioned Alex White to you. I don't know if he did, but Alex White who's not in good health now was ran the service organization at UCLA'

claims.claim: Hosea Williams co-led the Selma-to-Montgomery march with John Lewis on Bloody Sunday.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 113-116 (00:17:53-00:18:16): 'Hosea Williams would become one of my dearest, dearest closest friends'; 'he led that March on the Selma-to-Montgomery marches with John R. Lewis'
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Also corroborated by the civil_rights_facts.json Hosea Williams entry.

claims.claim: The SCLC board approved SCOPE in December 1964, targeting 120 counties.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 210-211 (00:36:50-00:37:21): 'Proved it in December and they targeted 120 counties.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated; the year is inferred from context (project ran summer 1965).

claims.claim: SCLC sought 2,000 volunteers for SCOPE but only got 500.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 227 (00:39:42): 'they wanted 2000 volunteers to man these 120 counties, they only got 500.'

claims.claim: The orientation week was held at Morehouse College in Atlanta.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 247 (00:42:49): 'we went to Atlanta. We were housed at Morehouse College in the dorms.'

claims.claim: Orientation speakers included Bayard Rustin, A. Philip Randolph, Hosea Williams, Norman Hill, C. Vann Woodward, John Hope Franklin, Lillian Smith, Septima Clark, Vernon Jordan, Charles Morgan Jr., Clarence Mitchell Jr., Michael Harrington, and John Doar.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 252-256 (00:43:56-00:45:07) lists all these speakers explicitly.

claims.claim: Dr. King's orientation speech named Muslims and Hindus alongside Jews and Gentiles.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 259-260 (00:45:30-00:45:55): 'When he spoke to us, knowing that there were young people, someone who might be atheists or agnostic, he said Muslims and Hindus and non-believers.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated; Leventhal cites the Morehouse College recording.

claims.claim: Leventhal was assigned to Bibb County (Macon, Georgia) and lived with the Simmons family in East Macon.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 291-292 (00:51:15-00:51:21): 'We got assigned to Bibb County, which was small, but we had the one of the biggest group for the 20.' Segment 354 (01:02:30): 'I ended up living with the Simmons family.'

claims.claim: William P. Randall was the Macon local SCLC leader and later became Bibb County Commissioner.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 296-297 (00:51:56-00:52:11): 'they had a great local leader, William P. Randall'; Segment 351 (01:01:47): 'He later became, there's a building name for him... He later became a Bibb County Commissioner.'

claims.claim: Leventhal played for the Macon Bombers, an integrated baseball team, as their only white player.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 444 (01:15:34): 'I had played there for the making bombers. I was only white making bombers.'

claims.claim: His Macon Bombers first game was at Round Oak, Georgia, on a cow-pasture baseball field.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 286 (00:50:41): 'my first baseball game with the Macon Bombers in Round Oak, Georgia, which was in the middle of a cow pasture that had been, trees had been cut down, and there were cow patties on the baseball field'

claims.claim: Leventhal was on base 11 out of 15 times across five Macon Bombers games.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 410 (01:09:16): 'It was, I was on base 11 out of 15 times, which is very, very good on base average.'

claims.claim: Luther Williams Field in Macon (1919) was used for filming dugout scenes in the 2013 Jackie Robinson film 42.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 445-446 (01:15:34-01:16:20): 'Luther Williams field, which was 1919 was one of the third oldest fields. That was used for the Jack Robinson film that just came out.'
claims.rationale: Transcript supports the field being used for filming, but does not specifically date the film as 2013. The summary inferred the film as 42 (2013) from context; this is canonical historical knowledge but slightly extends the transcript.

claims.claim: Brooklyn Dodgers played exhibition games in Macon, Birmingham, Montgomery, and Jacksonville on their way north from Vero Beach spring training.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 447 (01:16:20): 'Brooklyn Dodgers would do as a team is when they would come north out of Viral Beach, they would play in Birmingham, Montgomery, Jacksonville.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated; 'Viral Beach' is a transcription variant of 'Vero Beach.'

claims.claim: Leventhal was first arrested in Macon after being booked then released through William P. Randall's intervention with the police chief.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 487-527 (01:24:04-01:29:38): full arrest sequence, including police officer's 'nigger lover and smart aleck' charge, William P. Randall's confrontation with the police chief, and dropped charges.

claims.claim: John R. Lewis and Leventhal attempted to integrate the First Baptist Church in Americus, confronted by fire chief Henderson with a tape recorder.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 587-595 (01:41:42-01:43:13): full narrative, including Lewis's scripture quoting and Henderson's flustered response.

claims.claim: Andrew Whatley was killed in Americus shortly before Leventhal's arrival; the killers were two Black men.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 231-232 (00:40:15-00:40:37): 'a bystander who was white, who had Andrew Whatley, who I consider a martyr of the movement, who had just joined the Marines, was a bystander when they were throwing rocks at the marchers. And there was a black car. A car came with two black guys. They weren't part of the movement and they were tired of people being abused and they shot back.' Segment 580 (02:30:13): 'Andrew Whatley had just been killed before we got there.'

claims.claim: Leventhal was shot at twice in Americus, first in a Citroen driven by Fred Hoffman with Rich Adler as passenger.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 670-714 (02:08:48-02:06:37): full Citroen shooting narrative including Rich Adler's identification as a Grant High School gymnast.

claims.claim: Marcus Crane was the LA County Jail chief psychiatrist who also evaluated Sirhan Sirhan after the Robert F. Kennedy assassination.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 828 (02:26:43): 'The police chief was Peter J. Pitchess, who had been an FBI agent. And the FBI knew who I was and Marcus Crane, who said, Sirhan Sirhan was sane after he killed Robert F. Kennedy and said, I needed to be locked up in a mental hospital.'
claims.rationale: Transcript supports Crane as a jail psychiatrist who evaluated both Leventhal and Sirhan. However, transcript also says Crane told Leventhal HE 'needed to be locked up in a mental hospital. Because I worked for Martin Luther King' -- so Crane's view of Leventhal contradicts the framing 'wrote a hostile evaluation seeking commitment.' Summary correctly captures this. The specific phrasing of Crane's Sirhan view is potentially complicated.

claims.claim: Leventhal served as Western States college coordinator at WCLC headquartered at 2400 Southwestern Avenue, Suite 102, Los Angeles, California 90018.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 908 (02:40:50): '2400, 2400 Southwestern Avenue, Suite 102, Los Angeles, California, I know 018.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated; '018' is short for '90018' as ZIP code. Note: an earlier segment 906 had '2401' rather than '2400.'

claims.claim: Rev. Thomas Kilgore Jr. was president of WCLC, formerly of New York.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 900 (02:39:41): 'Western Christian Leadership Conference, Rev. Thomas Kilgore Jr, who had been in New York, was now out here. He was the president.' Segment 908: 'Thomas Kilgore, was the director Western Bureau SCLC, which was essentially W.'

claims.claim: Gwen Green was the WCLC executive director.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 805-806 (02:23:03-02:23:31), Segment 893-895 (02:38:50-02:39:01), Segment 912 (02:41:31): 'Gwen Green who ran the WCLC was there' (during summer 1965 wrap-up at West Hunter Street Baptist Church); 'When Green was the director of the director, she since told me that all the male ministers didn't make it that she had to fight as a woman.'

claims.claim: Leventhal was held 112 days at LA County Jail starting fall 1966.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 756 (02:15:54): '112 days. And I tell you, 112 days is not a long, long time in jail, but it's a pretty long time in jail.' Segment 1006 (02:58:24): 'three and a half months later or two hundred and twelve days later' (likely a transcription glitch where '212' should be '112'). Segment 770 (02:18:02): 'Somebody called and said they were going to bomb my place because you had black people there. This was this fall of 66.'
claims.rationale: 112 days directly stated; 1966 fall directly stated.

claims.claim: Hugo Maxi, a Nation of Islam-affiliated inmate on parole from Soledad, attacked Leventhal in LA County Jail with a razor-blade-embedded bar of soap.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 1003-1004 (02:57:41-02:58:09): 'Hugo Maxi. He was on parole, from from solid.' Earlier: 'He hit me with a bar soap with a razor blade and cut my eye.'
claims.rationale: Directly stated. 'Solid' is a transcription variant of 'Soledad' (state prison).

claims.claim: Reverend Marcus Moon ran for commissioner in Macon, September 15, 1965, the first Black candidate since Reconstruction.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 729 (02:09:54): 'The election was September 15th in Macon.' Segment 735 (02:11:07): 'it was the first black who had run since reconstruction. It was for water commissioners.'

claims.claim: On election night, Leventhal received a death-threat call: 'If you're still here in 24 hours, you're dead.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 783 (02:20:06): 'If you're still here in 24 hours, you did click. It was a very serious, it wasn't saying you're going to be better, be careful. You're here in 24 hours and you're dead.'

claims.claim: Leventhal critiques Pulitzer Prize-winning King biographers Taylor Branch and David Garrow for relying on disputed FBI files.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 145-156 (00:25:21-00:27:29) detail his critique of Garrow's 'slimy, sleazy book using Hoover's Files, which Taylor Branch also does.' Segment 832-836 (02:27:35-02:42:43) extends to Branch's pattern.
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Note: This is Leventhal's interpretation/polemic, not historical consensus.

claims.claim: Hodding Carter III wrote 'So the Heffners Left McComb' about Albert 'Red' Heffner's family.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 645-646 (02:42:42-02:59:00): 'It was Albert "Red" Heffner... the hot ink harder, the third, the one that worked for Jimmy Carter wrote a book called So the Heffners Left McComb.'
claims.rationale: Transcript contains the book title verbatim and identifies the author by description ('the one that worked for Jimmy Carter') -- which historically is Hodding Carter III. The 'hot ink harder, the third' is a Whisper rendering of 'Hodding Carter III.' Summary's attribution is historically correct but extends the transcript via inferred name.

claims.claim: Sammy Mahone is co-chair of the Americus-Sumter County Movement.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 609 (01:46:05): 'They have an organization which is very active. Samohoan is the co-chair.' ('Samohoan' is a Whisper rendering of 'Sammy Mahone.')

claims.claim: Stokely Carmichael near the end of his life told Louis Farrakhan not to attack Dr. King.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 843-847 (02:30:14-02:30:47): 'Stokely Carmichael himself or Stokely Carmichael himself when he had cancer in late in his life with with Lewis Farrakhan, Louis Farrakhan in Washington. It's on video. I've not seen it, but I've heard of it.'
claims.rationale: Leventhal explicitly states this is hearsay he has heard but not personally seen. The claim itself is Carmichael's reported reconciliation, which the summary correctly preserves with hedged framing.

claims.claim: Coretta Scott King had Leventhal author the first King federal holiday report to President Reagan and Congress.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 145 (00:25:21): 'I wrote the report to President Reagan and the Congress on the first King holiday for Coretta Scott King.' Segment 368-371 (01:04:38-01:05:02): describes the report and Lloyd Davis's role.

claims.claim: Andy Goodman, Mickey Schwerner, and James Chaney were the three civil rights workers killed in Mississippi (two Jewish); Andy Goodman attended Queens College.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 135 (00:23:01): 'Andy Goodman went to school. That's one of, one that was killed with James, James, Cheney and Mickey Schwerner.' Segment 102 (00:15:23): 'the three guys that were killed in Mississippi to a re-Jewish'
claims.rationale: All three names appear in transcript. The summary did not name all three in its main_summary but did imply two Jewish workers; chapter 2 summary mentions context.

claims.claim: Burke Marshall was the Kennedy administration assistant attorney general for civil rights.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 571 (01:37:05): 'I've talked to Burke Marshall.'
claims.rationale: Leventhal cites direct conversation with Burke Marshall (historically the AAG for Civil Rights 1961-1965).

publication_decision.rationale: The main_summary scored 84/100 accuracy and 86/100 quality -- both below the 90/90 publication threshold. The citation audit found 5 partially_supported claims out of 43 (including the Luther Williams Field film identification as 42 (2013), the Hodding Carter III book attribution, the Marcus Crane Sirhan assessment which collapses two contradictory Leventhal statements, the Stokely Carmichael/Farrakhan reconciliation claim which Leventhal explicitly identifies as hearsay, and a chapter-8 mistake where the summary called one of the four jailed Americus women 'Mary Bell Peacamble' when the transcript actually says 'Amy Peacamble'). Additionally the summary contains interpretive language about Taylor Branch and David Garrow that presents Leventhal's polemic as historical fact. Routing to human review is the correct outcome: a Smithsonian/LoC reviewer should (1) confirm the canonical 'Amy Peacamble' name spelling, (2) decide whether to hedge or remove the Crane/Sirhan framing given Leventhal's contradictory statements, (3) decide whether the polemic against Branch/Garrow should be retained as Leventhal's voice or removed/reframed, and (4) verify the film '42' (2013) attribution at Luther Williams Field via independent sources.
